entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - External logging to Datadog Using OpenTelemetry
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) with Software version update 8.42.* has introduced support for OpenTelemetry (OTEL) as an external logging destination, allowing you to stream Message Processing Logs (MPL) directly to OTEL-supported observability platfroms like Datadog, Grafana, Splunk etc for centralized observability.

What is OpenTelemetry? OpenTelemetry is a vendor-neutral framework for collecting, processing, and exporting telemetry data—metrics, logs, and traces. It's widely adopted across the industry and supported by most observability platforms, including Datadog. This standardized approach means your integration logs can be correlated with other systems and applications in your Datadog environment.

In this guide, I will walk through the step-by-step configuration needed to send CPI logs to Datadog using the OTEL integration, including prerequisites, BTP setup, and validation of the data flow.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

  • An active Datadog account with access to the organization settings
  • A Datadog API key (available at Organization Settings > API Keys)
  • BTP subaccount with Connectivity and Destination access
  • Acess in BTP subaccount to create/update service instance of Process Integration Runtime with appropriate roles

Step 1: Configure Your Datadog Setup

First, prepare your Datadog environment to receive logs from CPI.

Get Your API Key

Navigate to your Datadog organization settings and create an API key if you don't have one already. Go to Organization Settings and select API Keys. Create a new key and copy it—you'll need this for the BTP destination configuration.



Identify Your Datadog Region and OTEL Endpoint

Depending on where your Datadog organization is hosted, you'll use a different endpoint. For example:

  • EU region: https://otlp.datadoghq.eu/v1/logs
  • US region: https://otlp.datadoghq.com/v1/logs

Confirm your organization's base URL (it appears in your browser when you log in) and append the OTEL log endpoint accordingly.

Step 2: Create the BTP Destination

Now configure the HTTP destination in SAP BTP that will serve as the bridge between CPI and Datadog.

Navigate to Destinations

Log into SAP BTP, open your integration subaccount, and navigate to Connectivity > Destinations.

Create a New Destination

Click "Create Destination" and configure it with the following parameters:

Name: CloudIntegration_MonitoringDataConsumer
Type: HTTP
Authentication: No Authentication
URL: https://otlp.datadoghq.eu/v1/logs

Authentication set to "No Authentication"?  Yes—the Datadog API key will be passed as a header, not as basic auth.

Add Additional Properties

Scroll down and add the following additional properties. These fields tell Datadog how to classify and organize your CPI logs:

Property Name Value Purpose

OTELServiceNameMessageProcessingLogsIdentifies the service sending logs in Datadog
OTELScopeNameMPLLogical grouping identifier for CPI logs
OTELScopeVersion1.0.0Version of the log schema
URL.headers.dd-api-key<Your Datadog API Key>Authentication header for Datadog ingestion
TargetSystemOTELSpecifies OpenTelemetry as the target system

Important: Replace <Your Datadog API Key> with the actual API key you created earlier. Store this key securely and never commit it to version control.

After entering all properties, click Save.

Step 3: Activate External Logging in CPI

With the destination configured, you now need to activate external logging at the tenant level.

Create a Service Instance

In your BTP subaccount, go to Services > Instances and Subscriptions. Create a new instance of Process Integration Runtime with the following settings:

  • Service: Process Integration Runtime
  • Plan: api
  • Roles: ExternalLoggingActivate, ExternalLoggingActivationRead

Once created, generate a service key and copy the following credentials:

  • Client ID
  • Client Secret
  • Token URL
  • API Host (base URL for your CPI tenant)

Authenticate and Call the Activation API

Use Postman (or curl) to authenticate with the token URL and then call the external logging activation endpoint. Here's the flow:

1. Get an OAuth token:

POST {TokenURL}
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=client_credentials
&client_id={ClientID}
&client_secret={ClientSecret}

Copy the access_token from the response.

2. Activate external logging:

POST https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/activateExternalLogging?defaultLogLevel=INFO
Authorization: Bearer {access_token}

The defaultLogLevel parameter controls which log levels are sent. Options are:

  • NONE—disable external logging
  • INFO—send info, warn, and error logs (recommended)
  • ERROR—send error logs only

A successful response confirms external logging is now active. You should see logs flowing to Datadog within a few seconds.

Verify Activation Status

To confirm external logging is active, query the status endpoint:

GET https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/ExternalLoggingActivationStatus('{TenantName}')
Authorization: Bearer {access_token}

A successful response will show the activation status as true.

Step 4: Verify Data Flow and View Logs in Datadog

Once external logging is activated, CPI will begin sending Message Processing Logs to Datadog in real-time.

Querying Logs in Datadog

In Datadog, open the Logs section and search for logs with the service you configured in BTP Destination OTELServiceName.

service:MessageProcessingLogs

You should see MPL entries streaming into Datadog logs.

Parsing MPL Content as JSON

CPI sends MPL logs in a structured format, but the log content itself may be nested. To extract individual MPL attributes and create reports or dashboards, you can use Datadog's Pipelines feature:

Option 1: JSON Parser — If your log body is valid JSON, use the JSON parser to flatten the structure and extract fields. 

Option 2: GROK Parser — For more complex log formats, use a GROK parser pattern to extract specific fields.

Note: My Datadog was missing JSON parser, so I created 2 GROK Parsing rule as below after carfully verifing all the log content format ingested by SAP CI for various combination (ApplicationMessageId, SenderId, CustomStatus, LastError, LastErrorModelStepId etc) to parse all MPL logs.


Once parsed, you can facet logs by integration name, message ID, status, and other attributes to build dashboards, set up alerts, and troubleshoot issues faster.

Maintenance and Troubleshooting

Updating the Destination

If you need to modify the destination (e.g., change the API key or region), follow this sequence:

  1. Call the deactivateExternalLogging endpoint
  2. Update the destination in BTP
  3. Call the activateExternalLogging endpoint again

Skipping deactivation can result in not reflect the destinatinn updates.

Known Limitations

When CPI sends logs via OTEL, the timeUnixNano and observedTimeUnixNano fields are currently set to 0 instead of a proper Unix epoch value for custom headers. Datadog doesn't process these malformed timestamps, so log ingestion still works but only for Run Events and not Custom Headers. I believe SAP will address this in a future update.

Useful API Endpoints

For reference, here are other relevant endpoints for managing external logging:

Deactivate external logging:
POST https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/deactivateExternalLogging

View external logging events:
GET https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/ExternalLoggingEvents

Check MPL logs (alternative query):
GET https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/MessageProcessingLogs

For detailed API specifications, refer to the SAP API Hub documentation on External Logging.

entry.title: CPI Process Colorizer and Where used global search - Chrome extensions with the help of Vibe coding
entry.content:
When I started working on all of this, I was after two things:

  1. Make my life easier when refactoring complex flows with no proper documentation (Yes, that's happens).
  2. To see how far the unhinged genius of vibe coding can take me, considering how deeply AI started to integrate with daily routine. (Spoiler alert: pretty far. "Away" or not - it's discussible 😁)

Because once you see flow with 30+ subprocesses with names "Local integration Process 1 .. N", you inevitably start asking the Universe some questions. For example about:

  and  

 

 

Let's start with "Where used" global search as appetizer.

 

CPI Where Used rmbc

Link to Chrome Web Storage. Link to source code at Codeberg.

The idea is to find all mentions of selected text within all flows. It might greatly help, for example: when you are shifting from one Cloud connector to another and you need to change all Location Ids, urls, credentials and etc. and you have no idea which flow has it.

Select text on a page at your CPI tenant (both CF and Neo), right-click, choose "CPI Where Used rmbc" (rmbc = right mouse button click) and the extension opens a new tab with all iFlows where that exact text appears (checking credentials, pgp keys, urls, jms queus, script names and etc. including search in default values and externalized configured params.).

Why contextMenus ? Simply because I have enough buttons from different extensions.

Spoiler


How it works:
The extension:
1. Uses the current CPI tenant URL and existing browser session cookies.
2. Fetches a CSRF token.
3. Loads all CPI packages.
4. Loads artifacts for each package.
5. Filters artifacts to "IFlow".
6. Downloads each "iFlow detail JSON".
7. Recursively searches all string, number and boolean values for the selected text.
8. Opens a result tab showing package, iFlow, JSON path and snippet.

Around 200 flows took a little more than a minute to retrieve and parse.

Since I wanted to avoid redownload everything in case of need of new search within small period of time - caching is introduced.

Caching:
Package/artifact metadata and iFlow detail JSON are cached per tenant for 15 minutes inside the extension service worker. It is not persisted in chrome.storage. So repeating a search shortly after the previous one should avoid much of the repeated downloading and waiting, unless Chrome stops the service worker or the cache expires.

Limitations:
It's obviously can't find dynamically built queue names, since those generated names do not exist within the flow metadata.

It does not unzip full exported packages. As a result it's not parsing inside groovy scripts and etc., so Extension will not find function "ABC" if it's only not mentioned in attributes.
Hm... Idea...

 

And now the "main dish":

 

CPI Colorizer and Process Panel

Link to Chrome Web Storage. Link to source code at Codeberg.

This one visually colorizes SAP CPI processes and subprocess call boxes and shows a searchable process panel from the BPMN model. And most important thing: it navigate you in the model via pan to selected process or its' step. You don't need frantically search in which subprocess, under which label desired script exist.

How it works:
Two editor-only enhancements:

1. Process colorization
The extension reads the BPMN model of the currently opened iFlow and builds a process color matrix. Main Integration Processes, Local Integration Processes, and local process-call boxes are colored consistently. A call box receives the same color as the subprocess it calls, making process relationships easier to understand visually.

Spoiler

2. Searchable process panel
The extension injects an “iFlow Processes” panel into the CPI left navigation area. The panel lists processes, subprocesses, exception subprocesses, and steps in flow order. It includes search, expand all, collapse all and BPMN reload controls. Searching can find step names, process names, scripts, mapping names, tooltips, and configured step attributes.

Spoiler
   

Panel rows are clickable. Clicking a process or step pans/selects the corresponding object in the CPI model and highlights it with a temporary pulse outline.
8Mb gif under the spoiler.

Spoiler

 Honorable mention: gif was created by "Capture to a Gif" extension. Link to Chrome Web Storage

The extension includes a browser popup where users can configure:
- whether Color/Panel is enabled by default
- process/subprocess positioning
- step positioning

Spoiler

It's needed cause everyone is working under different screen resolution and has their own preferences. You can always toogle colorization and panel injection by floating button.

 

Now I will go full nerd mode with the code comments (functions with more than 10 lines would be under the spoiler)... You can skip it to PS: if you want.

What's going on at the editor page:

Side panel:
Custom Panel is injected as the last <li> item in a list of <ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>:

<ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>
  ...
  <li id="cpi-colorizer-side-panel" data-cpi-colorizer="process-panel">
    <!-- extension panel content -->
  </li>
</ul>

It was done intentionally, so it participates in the same navigation scrolling area as the native menu entries. The panel content itself does not use its own max-height or internal scrollbar.

Tried to inject it as separate block with fixed height and separate scroll bar, but with small screen resolution it would be very hard to use and scroll bars would be everywhere, since we would "eat" the space of standard Menu. So moved to "<ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>".

But in this case a bug was encountered: if expand/collapse standard menu injection is flickering. Reason is that SAPUI5 rerenders the list and temporarily removes injected <li>, later the watcher inserts it again.

setInterval(() => {
  ensureSidePanelInjected();
}, 1500);

To make it smooth, it was done via MutationObserver (provides the ability to watch for changes being made to the DOM tree) .

const host =
  document.getElementById("shell--sideNavigationMenu-Flexible-Content")
  || document.getElementById("shell--navigationList")?.parentElement;
// then
sidePanelObserver.observe(host, {
  childList: true,
  subtree: true
});

Checking if <div id="shell--sideNavigationMenu-Flexible-Content"> is available. If not - fallback to <ul id="shell--navigationList">.
When SAPUI5 expands/collapses menu items or rerenders the left navigation, the observer calls

function scheduleSidePanelEnsure() {
  if (sidePanelEnsureRaf) return;

  sidePanelEnsureRaf = requestAnimationFrame(() => {
    sidePanelEnsureRaf = null;
    ensureSidePanel();
  });
}

Executing function ensureSidePanel()

Spoiler
function ensureSidePanel() {
  const navigationList = document.getElementById("shell--navigationList");
  if (!navigationList) return false;

  let panel = document.getElementById(PANEL_ID);
  const wasCreated = !panel;
  if (!panel) {
    panel = createSidePanel();
    navigationList.appendChild(panel);
  } else if (panel.parentElement !== navigationList) {
    // SAPUI5 can rerender the left navigation. If preserved panel is still
    // present but no longer inside the current navigation <ul>, move it back.
    navigationList.appendChild(panel);
  }

  if (currentModel) {
    // Do not repaint the whole panel on every observer check.
    // Only ensure the panel exists after SAPUI5 rerenders the left navigation.
    const body = document.getElementById(PANEL_BODY_ID);
    if (wasCreated || !body || body.childElementCount === 0) {
      renderSidePanelTree(getSidePanelSearchValue());
    }
  } else {
    loadSidePanelModel();
  }

  return true;
}

But the biggest time eater was navigation and positioning. 


Navigation and positioning:
First idea was to navigate SVG nodes

[id*="CallActivity_63"]
[data-sap-ui*="CallActivity_63"]
[aria-label*="CallActivity_63"]

Then just scroll into view

element.scrollIntoView({
  block: "center",
  inline: "center"
});

And emulate mouse events

element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("mousedown", { bubbles: true }));
element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("mouseup", { bubbles: true }));
element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("click", { bubbles: true }));

But that fails. Scrolling was not working at all and diagram was hysterically moved by emulating clicks.

The cause is that CPI has its own pan control with buttons

To find them we need in Chrome Dev Tools (F12) in console tab

[...document.querySelectorAll("*")]
  .filter(el => {
    const text = [
      el.getAttribute("title"),
      el.getAttribute("aria-label"),
      el.textContent
    ]
      .filter(Boolean)
      .join(" ")
      .toLowerCase();

    return text.includes("pan view");
  });

Dumping results to the AI helps a lot to solve the main problem: that clicking emulation not only trigger standard movement behavior but also triggering positioning of the bottom property panel, that changing the size of the box where diagram is.

So solution is "pan" first to the specific anchoring values and only after that "select". Starting this flow by

Spoiler
/**  Entry point for panel click navigation to a Process_* or SubProcess_* or step BPMN id. */
function focusRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) {
  const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
  if (!target) {
    setSidePanelStatus(`Could not find rendered element ${bpmnId}. Try Reload BPMN model.`);
    return;
  }

  // Selection itself makes CPI/Galilei auto-pan the model. That auto-pan fights
  // with our own pan calculation, so navigation now does the opposite order:
  // first use Galilei's pan buttons to move the target close to our desired
  // viewport anchor, then select it. When the element is already visible in a
  // good position, CPI's selection has little or no reason to move the view.
  const runId = ++panelNavigationRunId;
  lastPanelNavigation = { bpmnId, timestamp: Date.now() };
  preAnchorThenSelectModelElement(bpmnId, runId);
}

 Then find the real SVG object like:

Spoiler
CallActivity_63  → BPMNShape_CallActivity_63
SubProcess_16    → BPMNShape_SubProcess_16
Process_1        → BPMNShape_Participant_Process_1
/**
 * Finds the exact rendered BPMNShape_* SVG group for a BPMN model id.
 */
function findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) {
  const id = String(bpmnId || "");
  if (!id) return null;

  const diagramId = currentModel?.shapeDiagramIdById?.get(id);
  const candidateIds = uniqueStrings([
    diagramId,
    `BPMNShape_${id}`,
    normalizeProcessId(id) ? `BPMNShape_Participant_${id}` : ""
  ]);

  for (const candidateId of candidateIds) {
    const element = document.getElementById(candidateId);
    if (element && !isExtensionElement(element)) return element;
  }

  // Fallback only. Some CPI builds expose the id in sap-automation or other attributes.
  // Restrict to Galilei/SVG nodes so our own side-panel rows are never returned.
  const escapedId = cssEscape(id);
  return document.querySelector(
    `svg .sapGalileiSymbolNode[id$="${escapedId}"], ` +
    `svg g[sap-automation*="${escapedId}"], ` +
    `svg g[id*="${escapedId}"]`
  );
}

Key function is preAnchorThenSelectModelElement() that is moving view in steps with tick of 22 ms

Spoiler
/**
 * Pans the target into place first, then pulses and selects it in CPI.
 */
function preAnchorThenSelectModelElement(bpmnId, runId) {
  // Run enough pan passes before selecting so a single panel click can cover
  // far-away elements. Earlier versions only did a small number of pan steps,
  // so users had to click the same panel row repeatedly. Galilei pan buttons
  // update the SVG asynchronously, therefore each pass does one small pan and
  // waits briefly before measuring again. The short delay keeps long-distance
  // jumps responsive while still letting Galilei apply each pan step.
  let pass = 0;
  let stableNearAnchorTicks = 0;
  let lastPositionSignature = "";
  const maxPasses = 120;

  const tick = () => {
    if (runId !== panelNavigationRunId || lastPanelNavigation.bpmnId !== bpmnId) return;
    const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
    if (!target) return;

    const signature = getRenderedElementAnchorSignature(bpmnId);
    if (signature && signature === lastPositionSignature) {
      stableNearAnchorTicks += isRenderedModelElementNearAnchor(bpmnId) ? 1 : 0;
    } else {
      stableNearAnchorTicks = isRenderedModelElementNearAnchor(bpmnId) ? 1 : 0;
      lastPositionSignature = signature;
    }

    if (stableNearAnchorTicks < 2 && pass < maxPasses) {
      anchorRenderedModelElementWithGalileiPan(bpmnId, { pulse: false, maxIterations: 1 });
      pass += 1;
      setTimeout(tick, 22);
      return;
    }

    const finalTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) || target;
    pulseRenderedModelElement(finalTarget);
    dispatchSyntheticEditorClick(finalTarget);

    // Re-pulse after CPI opens the standard properties area. Do not pan after
    // selection: CPI may auto-adjust the viewport while selecting the object,
    // and a second extension pan would fight that internal movement.
    setTimeout(() => {
      if (runId !== panelNavigationRunId || lastPanelNavigation.bpmnId !== bpmnId) return;
      const selectedTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
      if (selectedTarget) pulseRenderedModelElement(selectedTarget);
    }, 450);
  };

  tick();
}

Then just check if we are close enough to selection event.

Spoiler
function anchorRenderedModelElementWithGalileiPan(bpmnId, options = {}) {
  const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
  if (!(target instanceof Element)) return;

  const viewport = findGalileiViewport(target);
  const controls = findGalileiPanControls();
  if (!viewport || !controls.left || !controls.right || !controls.up || !controls.down) return;

  const maxIterations = Number.isFinite(options.maxIterations) ? options.maxIterations : 12;

  for (let i = 0; i < maxIterations; i += 1) {
    const latestTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) || target;
    const targetRect = latestTarget.getBoundingClientRect?.();
    const viewportRect = viewport.getBoundingClientRect?.();

    const anchor = getNavigationAnchorFractions(bpmnId);
    const desiredLeft = viewportRect.left + viewportRect.width * anchor.x;
    const desiredTop = viewportRect.top + viewportRect.height * anchor.y;

    const dx = targetRect.left - desiredLeft;
    const dy = targetRect.top - desiredTop;

    const needsX = Math.abs(dx) > 90;
    const needsY = Math.abs(dy) > 70;
    if (!needsX && !needsY) break;

    if (needsX) {
      clickBestPanButton(
        latestTarget,
        Math.abs(dx),
        dx > 0 ? controls.right : controls.left,
        dx > 0 ? controls.left : controls.right,
        "x",
        anchor
      );
    }

    if (needsY) {
      clickBestPanButton(
        latestTarget,
        Math.abs(dy),
        dy > 0 ? controls.down : controls.up,
        dy > 0 ? controls.up : controls.down,
        "y",
        anchor
      );
    }
  }
}

But it also checks if pan is inverted via clickBestPanButton()

Spoiler
/**
 * Chooses and clicks the most effective Galilei pan button for one axis.
 */
function clickBestPanButton(target, previousDistance, primaryButton, fallbackButton, axis, anchor = { x: 0.45, y: 0.45 }) {
  const beforeRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  clickGalileiPanButton(primaryButton);
  const afterRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  if (!isUsableRect(beforeRect) || !isUsableRect(afterRect)) return;

  // If the target did not move or moved clearly in the wrong direction, undo
  // with the opposite control and use that opposite control once. This keeps
  // the function robust if SAP's tooltip wording is interpreted differently.
  const moved = axis === "x" ? afterRect.left - beforeRect.left : afterRect.top - beforeRect.top;
  if (Math.abs(moved) < 0.5) return;

  // The caller passes previousDistance only as a guard against clearly bad
  // moves. Recompute against the viewport anchor for a reliable comparison.
  const viewport = findGalileiViewport(target);
  const viewportRect = viewport?.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  const newRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  if (!isUsableRect(viewportRect) || !isUsableRect(newRect)) return;
  const desired = axis === "x"
    ? viewportRect.left + viewportRect.width * anchor.x
    : viewportRect.top + viewportRect.height * anchor.y;
  const newDistance = Math.abs((axis === "x" ? newRect.left : newRect.top) - desired);

  if (newDistance > previousDistance + 20 && fallbackButton) {
    clickGalileiPanButton(fallbackButton);
    clickGalileiPanButton(fallbackButton);
  }
}



PS:

It's already passed the point of TL;DR , so pretty short:

Hope those extensions would help at least someone.

Of course, I do not collect any data with it. You can always check source code by provided links and by "%LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Extensions" path.

AI is obviously unbelievably strong helper but still, unfortunately, not perfect, "Where used" extension was written within two chill hours at the weekend watching football. "Colorizer and Panel" took quite a lot of time because of constant AI attempts to standardize, ignore problems and breaking what was working to solve non-working stuff 😁.  But, in any case, it saves hours and hours of work.


If you have any questions or encountered some bugs (it should be there 🤣) always welcome to write there in the comments, codeberg, email and etc.

entry.title: Understanding Read Lock Strategy in SMB Sender and Receiver Adapters
entry.content:
In file-based integrations, one of the most common challenges is ensuring that files are processed only after the write operation is fully complete. If a file is picked up while the source system is still writing to it, or if a target system accesses a file while it is still being written by SAP Integration Suite, it can lead to partial payload consumption, file corruption, and downstream processing failures. 

This challenge becomes even more critical when we do not have direct access to the source or target directory to validate how file creation is being handled externally. In such scenarios, the integration flow must rely on adapter-level mechanisms to ensure file stability. 

Consider an invoice-processing integration. 

An external source finance application writes large invoice payloads to a shared SMB directory. SAP Integration Suite polls this directory, processes the payload, applies transformations, and writes the output to another SMB location where the target application consumes it. 

Now imagine two possible situations: 

If SAP picks up a file while the source system is still writing data into it, the integration flow processes an incomplete payload. 

Similarly, if the target application reads the output file while SAP is still writing it, the file may appear malformed or incomplete, resulting in processing failures and incorrect or rejected data at the target system. 

To address this, the SMB Adapter in SAP Integration Suite provides a Read Lock Strategy for both Sender and Receiver adapters. 

Although the end result is the same, ensuring only stable files are processed, the implementation logic differs significantly between sender-side file consumption and receiver-side file writing. 

Read Lock Strategy in the SMB Sender Adapter 

On the sender side, the objective is to ensure that the adapter polls and consumes only fully committed files from the source directory. 

The default read lock option is None. 

In this mode, the adapter consumes the file immediately when detected during polling. This works well only if the external source system fully manages the integrity of the file during creation. 

However, many source systems write directly into the monitored directory. In such cases, the file may become visible to the adapter before the writing is complete. 

To handle this, the Content Change strategy can be used. 

Here, the adapter continuously monitors the file’s last modified timestamp and waits until the file remains unchanged for the configured Content Change Duration (in seconds). 

The validation logic is:  
Current Time - Last Modified Time > Configured Wait Time specified by Content Change Duration field.

In simple terms, the adapter checks whether sufficient time has passed since the last modification. 

If the elapsed time is greater than the configured Content Change Duration, the file is considered stable and ready for processing. 

Configuring this correctly ensures that the source system has completed its write operation before the file is picked up for further processing. 

Read Lock Strategy in the SMB Receiver Adapter 

On the receiver side, the focus is on ensuring that the target system can access the file only after the write operation is complete. The objective here is to ensure that the target system sees the file only after SAP Integration Suite has completed writing to it. 

This read lock strategy is available when Existing File Handling is configured as Override, Fail, or Ignore.

For the read lock strategy, you can choose None, which does not apply any file locking mechanism during file creation. 

Alternatively, the Rename option uses a write-to-temp-first approach. 

Instead of writing directly to the target file, the adapter first writes to a uniquely generated temporary file based on the configured pattern in the Temporary File Name field. 

For example, if the final file name is: Demo.txt and the Temporary File Name is configured as target_${exchangeId}.temp, the adapter creates a file as Demo.txt_AGoK7j2uHH0IrL7dUmZ6wGIR0Omr.temp

 The exchangeId-based suffix ensures uniqueness and avoids conflicts during concurrent processing. Alternatively, you can achieve the same result by using custom formats.

Once the write operation to the temporary file is complete, the adapter checks whether the target file already exists and evaluates the configured Existing File Handling behavior. 

Based on this evaluation, the adapter either renames the temporary file to the final file name, ignores the operation, or fails the processing step, depending on the configured Existing File Handling option. 

This write-to-temp-first approach ensures that the target system never encounters a partially written file. The file becomes available to the target application only after the write operation is fully complete. 

Business Impact 

In file exchange scenarios where file integrity is critical, such as invoice processing, payroll transfers, financial settlements, or master-data synchronization, incomplete files can lead to processing failures, data mismatches, and additional manual effort to correct them.

By enabling sender-side and receiver-side read lock strategies appropriately, file-based integrations become more reliable, reducing operational failures and supporting stable processing for enterprise-scale workloads.

entry.title: How to Link Attachments Between SuccessFactors Modules Using SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
In SAP SuccessFactors integrations, there are many business scenarios where attachments need to be transferred between different modules such as Recruiting to Onboarding or Onboarding to Employee Central and other modules. One common requirement is transferring offer letter attachments from the Recruiting module to the Onboarding module during the hiring process.

Although SAP SuccessFactors provides standard OData APIs for attachment handling, implementing this requirement using the SAP SuccessFactors adapter in SAP Integration Suite involves multiple steps. Due to security and module level data separation in SAP SuccessFactors, attachments are not directly shared across modules. Instead, attachments are stored independently and linked using unique attachment IDs.

In this blog post, we will go through the complete step-by-step process of retrieving an offer letter attachment from Recruiting and linking it to the Onboarding module using the SAP SuccessFactors adapter in SAP Integration Suite.

The first step is to retrieve the offer letter attachment details from the Recruiting module in Cloud Integration using SuccessFactors adapter. Connect SuccessFactors adapter with OData V2 and retrieve the offer letter from JobApplication entity as below

Once you have the offer letter retrieved, filter for the latest offer letter if there are multiple letter attached for the same candidate. This can be done using lastModifiedDateTime/offerLetterId/createdDateTime fields to filter

Attachments cannot be directly transferred from one SuccessFactors module to another, for example from Recruiting to Onboarding, due to security and ownership restrictions within SAP SuccessFactors. If you attempt to directly reuse the same attachment ID across modules, you may encounter authorization or access rights errors because the owner and security context of the attachment in Recruiting are different from those in Onboarding.

To overcome this limitation, the attachment must first be retrieved from the source module, then uploaded again to generate a new attachment ID in the target module. Finally, the newly generated attachment ID must be linked to the employee profile in the Onboarding module.

Prepare payload to upload the attachment from Recruiting to general attachment entity in Onboarding 

Use content modifier to prepare the payload like below. Get file content, file name from Recruiting (JobApplication Entity) and store them in property to use them in the payload. The module name in Recruiting is RECRUITING and when we upload into Onboarding, it's GENERIC_OBJECT. UserId is the ID which is used to connect SuccessFactors with Integration Suite.

<Attachment>
<Attachment>
<fileContent>${property.fileContent}</fileContent>
<fileName>${property.fileName}</fileName>
<module>GENERIC_OBJECT</module>
<userId>SFAPI</userId>
<viewable>true</viewable>
</Attachment>
</Attachment>

Upload Attachment to General Attachment Entity 

Connect with SuccessFactors Adapter with request reply and use Upsert (POST) Operation to post the payload prepared in previous step to attachment entity. 

Once attachment is uploaded into generic object, store the attachmentId from the response payload 

Prepare payload to map the attachmentId to respective employee profile in Onboarding

Map the payload to target structure to upload the attachmentId

Note: We do not have to upload the file content, file name again. Instead we can link the attachmentId to employee profile in Onboarding

Upload attachmentId to MDF Object which is created in Onboarding to upload the attachments
Connect with SuccessFactors Adapter with request reply and use Upsert (POST) Operation to link the attachmentId 

Below is the response message after uploading the attachmentId successfully

<?xml version='1.0' encoding='utf-8'?><UpsertResponses><cust_ONB_NewHireDocumentsUpsertResponse><key>cust_ONB_NewHireDocuments/externalCode=100110</key><status>OK</status><editStatus>UPSERTED</editStatus><message null="true"/><index type="Edm.Int32">0</index><httpCode type="Edm.Int32">200</httpCode><inlineResults type="Bag(SFOData.UpsertResult)"/></cust_ONB_NewHireDocumentsUpsertResponse></UpsertResponses>

Reference:
Linking Attachments to an MDF Entity

entry.title: Connect SAP S/4HANA Odata Service Directly with SAP APIM with Oauth2.0 Based Authentication
entry.content:
Direct integration between SAP S/4HANA OData Services and SAP API Management using OAuth 2.0 enables secure and efficient API communication without routing through SAP CPI. This approach helps organizations simplify their architecture while maintaining centralized API security and governance.

In this blog, we will cover how to configure SAP API Management to securely expose SAP S/4HANA OData services using OAuth 2.0 authentication.


Prerequisites

  • SAP Integration Suite with API Management capability enabled
  • SAP Cloud Connector configured
  • SAP S/4HANA OData service activate

I am assuming that you have enabled API Management capability in Integration Suite.

Step 1 : Create API Provider

Connection

Type

On Premise

Host

Virtual Host, as mentioned in Cloud Connector

Port

Virtual Port, as mentioned in Cloud Connector

Location ID

As mentioned in Cloud Connector

Authentication

NONE

Additional Properties:

sap-client: XXX

Catalog Service Settings

Path Prefix

/sap/opu/odata

Service Collection URL

/IWFND/CATALOGSERVICE;v=2/ServiceCollection

Authentication type

BASIC

Username

SAP's User Name for communication

Password

***

After Configuring the above you can perform Test Connection I have also Attached screenshot for your reference.

Step 2 :  Create Key Value Map to store the credentials to S/4HANA(Click on Encrypted)

Step 3 : Click on Create API . Select the API Provider that we have configured Earlier and Click on Discover.

Step 4 : After Clicking on Discover select the Name and click on ok. Once you click ok then proxy will be created . 

Step 5 : Create a Subscription for the Product created.

Step 6 : The Go the the Policies tab.  In Proxyendpoint in PreFlow apply OAuthv2 Policy.
For Oauth2 Authentication setup in detail  you are refer my below blog
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/configuring-oauth-2-0-authentication-between-sap-api-management-and-sap/qaq-p/14275375#M4932247 

Oauth Policy

<OAuthV2 async="false" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
   <ExternalAuthorization>false</ExternalAuthorization>
   <Operation>VerifyAccessToken</Operation>
   <GenerateResponse enabled="true"/>
   <Tokens/>
</OAuthV2>

In proxyEndpoint Postflow I have implemented AssignMessage Policy.

Assign Message – If you encounter the error {"fault":{"faultstring":"Unsupported Encoding \"br\"","detail":{"errorcode":"protocol.http.UnsupportedEncoding"}}}, it's because BR (Brotli) is a compression format typically used by backends for webpage loading, and it is not supported here. Using the Assign Message policy helps remove this unsupported encoding so the message can reach the target successfully.

<AssignMessage async="false" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns='http://www.sap.com/apimgmt'>
    <Remove>
        <Headers>
            <Header name="Authorization"/>
        </Headers>
    </Remove>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>false</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
    <AssignTo createNew="false" type="request">request</AssignTo>
</AssignMessage>

Step 7 : In Target Endpoint in Postflow I have added Key value map policy and Basic Auth Policy.

Policy Key Value Map 

<KeyValueMapOperations mapIdentifier="s4hana" async="true" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <Get assignTo="private.basic_user" index="1">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>s4user</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Get assignTo="private.basic_password" index="1">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>s4password</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Scope>environment</Scope>
</KeyValueMapOperations>

Step 8  : Apply Basic Auth Policy.

Basic Auth 

<BasicAuthentication async='true' continueOnError='false' enabled='true' xmlns='http://www.sap.com/apimgmt'>
    <Operation>Encode</Operation>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>true</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
    <User ref='private.basic_user'></User>
    <Password ref='private.basic_password'></Password>
    <AssignTo createNew="false">request.header.Authorization</AssignTo>
</BasicAuthentication>

Step 9 : Click on Deploy

Step 10 : Time to test.  

Select authentication as Oauth2 and get the token.

Endpoint:  The Proxy Endpoint

GET /ProductMasterSet?$filter=FilterDate eq '20251023'

Description: Retrieves product master data filtered by the specified date.

Request Example

GET https://xyz-apim.test.apimanagement.in30.hana.ondemand.com:443/ProductMasterSet?$filter=FilterD… eq '20251023'

Headers: Accept: application/json Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

Conclusion

In this blog, we explored how to directly integrate SAP S/4HANA OData Services with SAP API Management using OAuth 2.0 authentication. We covered API Provider configuration, OAuth policy setup, Key Value Map implementation, and secure API exposure.

Using this approach, organizations can simplify integrations, centralize API security, and securely expose SAP S/4HANA services without routing requests through SAP CPI.

Reference
https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/create-api-provider
https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/setting-up-oauth-for-cloud-integration-in-cloud-foundry
https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/create-key-value-map

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI
entry.content:
"The iflow kafka topic routing per domain is immutable. The metadata is the architecture."

Introduction

If you have worked with SAP PI/PO or even with SAP CPI IDoc routing, you already know the pattern: one receiver, one router, metadata drives the decision. The artifact never changes — the configuration does.

This blog will support you about IDOC:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice

The same principle applies to Kafka but when teams start integrating Kafka topics into SAP CPI, the most common approach creates one iFlow per topic, one deployment per event type, one change request per new business event. A Sales O2C domain with 5 event types becomes 5 iFlows, 5 deployments, and 5 maintenance points. When the topic naming changes or a new event is added — a developer is required.

This post presents a pattern that eliminates that overhead. One Kafka Sender Adapter. One router iFlow. One Value Mapping that grows. All iFlows inside the domain package are immutable.

This is not a workaround. This is ODCP applied to event-driven architecture in SAP CPI over the Domain Layer that lives above the tecnology.

The pattern was validated end-to-end with:

  • Confluent Cloud free tier as the Kafka broker
  • SAP BTP Integration Suite (CPI) standard Kafka Sender Adapter — no custom adapter, no additional cost
  • AVRO binary deserialization via Groovy Script using Apache Avro 1.12.1
  • Three input scenarios validated: raw bytes (production), Base64, and Hex (simulation/testing)
  • Confluent Wire Format detection (magic byte 0x00 + schema ID) handled automatically

⚠ This pattern is designed within the SDIA domain-centric governance framework using ODCP semantic name convention contract. If your organisation does not follow SDIA/ODCP, adapt the package names, iFlow names, and VM artifact names to your convention. The routing logic and Groovy script work independently of naming.

⚠ Scope Notice

The implementation demonstrated in this blog represents a foundational template — a minimal, working proof of concept designed to illustrate the immutability principle applied to Kafka topic routing in SAP CPI.

Schema design, field mapping, error handling strategy, retry policy, DLQ configuration, security hardening, and output format are intentionally kept simple. Every adaptation required for your specific landscape — domain model, event contract, operational SLA — is left to the implementing team.

The intent of this blog is not to deliver a production-ready solution. The intent is to demonstrate that one Kafka Sender Adapter, one router iFlow, and one Value Mapping are sufficient to implement a fully governed, domain-scoped, immutable routing mechanism in SAP CPI — without a single line of hardcode and without a custom adapter.

The principle is the deliverable. The implementation is your decision.

What is SDIA and why does it matter here ?

SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture) is a vendor-agnostic governance model built on one invariant: the business domain is the primary key of every artifact, from the API gateway to the data layer.

ODCP (Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern) is Layer 3 of SDIA and governs SAP BTP Integration Suite directly for SAP CI - Cloud Integration — one package per domain and subdomain ( other possible levels of granulality ), every artifact carries the domain token in a deterministic name, credentials segmented and etc..

In this pattern, the Kafka router iFlow is the domain boundary entry point — immutable, owned by the domain team, zero shared dependencies. The Value Mapping is the metadata contract. The processing iFlows are independently owned and deployed.

The same invariant applies at every layer of SDIA:

API Management  → GDCR/DDCR → KVM grows.     Proxy never changes.
SAP CPI IDoc    → ODCP      → VM grows.      iFlow never changes.
SAP CPI Kafka   → ODCP      → VM grows.      iFlow never changes.

The Architecture

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SDIA — Kafka Topic Domain Routing Architecture (Pattern: ODCP / L3)   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
 [ Confluent Cloud ] 
        │  (All topics: acme.sales.o2c.*)
        ▼
 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │ LAYER 1: KAFKA RECEIVER LAYER (Static iFlow)                         │
 │ iFlow: id00.o2c.kafka.topic.routing.f.in.async                       │
 ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
 │  1. processData()  ──► Reads 'kafka_receivedTopic' from header       │
 │  2. VM Lookup      ──► Matches topic in 'id00.o2c.vm.metadata'       │
 │                        └─► Success: sets ${target_channel}           │
 └──────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                         ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                   [ Mapped? ]           [ No ]──► [ Error End ] (500)
                         │ Yes
                         ▼
             〔 ProcessDirect Internal 〕
                         │
        ┌────────────────┼────────────────┬────────────────┐
        ▼                ▼                ▼                ▼
   /.../created     /.../confirmed   /.../cancelled   /.../invoice-created
 ┌──────┴────────────────┴────────────────┴────────────────┴────────────┐
 │ LAYER 2: DOMAIN PROCESSING LAYER (Independent Ownership)             │
 │ iFlow: id01.o2c.kafka.[event_type] (Template-based logic)            │
 ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
 │  • AVRO Deserialization  • Message Mapping     • Content Enrichment  │
 │  • Schema Validation     • JMS / DLQ Retry      • Target Adapter     │
 └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Why This Is Not Overengineering

This question deserves an honest answer — because in some scenarios, it genuinely is.

When it IS overengineering:

If your domain has 2 or 3 stable topics that never change, creating a router with a Value Mapping adds a layer with zero return. One iFlow per topic — simple, direct, maintainable.

When it is NOT overengineering:

When the domain is alive — new business events are added frequently, topic naming evolves, or multiple teams contribute to the same domain. In that scenario:

 
Without router:  New event → new iFlow + new adapter config + new credential + deploy  
With router:  New event → 1 row in Value Mapping → done. Zero deploy. Zero code.

The Value Mapping becomes the live catalog of every event the domain knows and processes. That is not overhead — that is governance.

The question that defines the decision:

"How often do new events appear in this domain?"

Domain volatility Design
Rare — stable topicsDirect adapter per topic
Frequent — growing eventsRouter + VM · immutable iFlow
UnknownRouter + VM · safe by default

The Routing Key — Kafka Topic as Semantic Contract

Unlike IDoc routing where the key is assembled from multiple header fields, Kafka routing is simpler and more elegant. The topic name is the semantic contract. It already carries domain, subdomain, and event intent:

acme.sales.o2c.order.created
  │      │    │    │      │
  │      │    │    │      └── event: what happened
  │      │    │    └───────── detail: which process
  │      │    └────────────── subdomain: which process
  │      └─────────────────── domain: which area
  └────────────────────────── company

One field. One lookup. One result. No composite key assembly needed.

Value Mapping Configuration

Artifact: id00.o2c.vm.metadata

The AVRO Schema — Externalized by Design

The AVRO schema is stored as a CPI Externalized Parameter — not hardcoded inside the Groovy script. This is not optional — it is the only acceptable design:

Hardcoded schema  → schema changes → script change → deployment required
Externalized      → schema changes → update parameter → zero deployment

The iFlow remains immutable. The schema evolves outside it.

Configure under the iFlow Externalized Parameters:

Name  : avro
Value : { "type": "record", "name": "Order", ... }

The Groovy Scripts

Iflow routing Kafka Topic inside the Domain Layer for Sales - O2C - CI Package:

As you can see the adapter Kafka is reading all topics regards Sales - O2C - Process:

Topics: acme.sales.o2c.order.created,acme.sales.o2c.order.confirmed...

Don't forget to add the Allowed Headers: kafka.TOPIC, this is the unic reference for the routing.

Confluence Kafka - Topics - Free Tier:

Create your own Kafka instance in 3 minutes and test it:https://www.confluent.io/get-started/

Iflow processing topic inside the package Sales O2C for the topic as sample

Kafka Topic for:acme.sales.o2c.order.created - Iflow to process: id01.o2c.kafka.order.created

Postman:

Result in XML:

Before and After

Traditional This pattern

New event typeNew iFlow + adapter + deployAdd 1 VM row
New topicNew iFlow + credential + deployAdd 1 VM row
Schema changeEdit Groovy + deployUpdate Externalized Parameter
Routing logicHardcoded per iFlowValue Mapping only
TraceabilityPer-iFlow MPLvm_key in every MPL log
iFlow count (5 events)5 iFlows with adapters1 router + 5 processing

Conclusion

The Kafka adapter never changes. The Value Mapping grows. The schema never touches the iFlow. The Externalized Parameter evolves.

Applying SDIA and ODCP to SAP Cloud Integration, you eliminate three of the most common failure modes in enterprise integration landscapes:

  • Package Sprawl → one package per domain. period.
  • Naming Chaos → every artifact carries the domain token. no exceptions.
  • Artifact Sprawl → one router iFlow per domain. processing iFlows are templates. Value Mapping is the only thing that grows.

Same principle. Layer 3 (ODCP) of the same architecture — SDIA.

SDIA and ODCP are open conceptual patterns created to reduce artifact sprawl and improve domain governance across SAP Integration Suite landscapes.

When your Sales O2C domain adds a new event type — acme.sales.o2c.shipment.dispatched — the steps are:

  1. Create the processing iFlow id01.o2c.kafka.shipment.dispatched
  2. Add one row to id00.o2c.vm.metadata
  3. Deploy the processing iFlow only.

The router iFlow id00.o2c.kafka.topic.routing.f.in.async is never touched.That is the contract.

This is not a CPI trick. This is not overengineering. This is what governance looks like when the domain is the primary key —
and every artifact knows exactly what it owns and what it does not.

  • The iFlow is the contract.
  • The Value Mapping is the configuration.
  • The domain is the boundary.
  • They must never be confused.

Resources

  • Script 1-Router Guard - Appendix - A
  • Script 2-Processing Iflow - Appendix - B
  • Required JAR - Appendix - C
  • Sample of avro and schema - Appendix D

Resources - SAP SCN Blog's

  • What You Need to Know About the Kafka Adapter SAP Blog (Official) · March 2021
  • Test Kafka Scenarios Using SAP CPI SAP Community · May 2021
  • Kafka Adapter with AVRO Serialization and Schema Registry - SAP Community · June 2022
  • SAP Integration Suite — Connect Everything Featuring Kafka SAP Community · March 2025
  • Receiver Kafka Adapter Scenario with AVRO Serialization and Schema Registry SAP Community · May 2026

Ricardo Viana
Independent Solo Researcher
Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP
ORCID: 0009-0009-9549-5862

  • ODCP v3.0: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593
  • SDIA v3.0: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635
  • SITE : www.domain-intent.com

Appendix - A

Script 1 — Router Gate - Inside the Spoiler

Spoiler
// =============================================================================
//  SAP Community Blog — Draft
//  title   : Domain-Driven Kafka Routing in SAP CPI using Value Mapping
//  author  : Ricardo Viana — SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
//  version : 1.0.0
//  date    : 2026-05-22
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  pattern : SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
//  purpose : reads kafka topic header, performs vm lookup, sets target_channel
//            for processdirect routing within the same integration package
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  functions exposed:
//    processData(message)       — reads kafka topic, calls vm, sets target_channel
//    buildErrorPayload(message) — replaces body with json error on failure
// =============================================================================

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory
import com.sap.it.api.mapping.ValueMappingApi

// =============================================================================
//  function : processData
// =============================================================================
def Message processData(Message message) {

    def vm_agency_src='sales.o2c.kafka'
    def vm_id_src='kafka.topic'
    def vm_agency_tgt = 'acme.enterprise.division'
    def vm_id_tgt     = 'acme.br.pd.o2c.iflow'
    def fallback      = 'unknown'

    def props   = message.getProperties()
    def headers = message.getHeaders()

    // ── reads kafka topic from header ─────────────────────────────────────────
    def kafka_topic    = (headers['kafka.TOPIC'] as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def target_channel = fallback
    def vm_error       = ''

    try {
        def vm_api = ITApiFactory.getApi(ValueMappingApi.class, null)

        def mapped = vm_api.getMappedValue(
            vm_agency_src, vm_id_src, kafka_topic,
            vm_agency_tgt, vm_id_tgt
        )

        if (mapped?.trim()) target_channel = mapped.trim()

    } catch (Exception e) {
        vm_error = e.message ?: 'unknown error'
    }

    // ── output properties ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    props.put('target_channel', target_channel)
    props.put('is_mapped',      (target_channel != fallback).toString())
    props.put('vm_key',         kafka_topic)
    props.put('vm_error',       vm_error)
    headers.put('vm_key',       kafka_topic)

    return message
}

// =============================================================================
//  function : buildErrorPayload
// =============================================================================
def Message buildErrorPayload(Message message) {

    def props          = message.getProperties()
    def target_channel = (props['target_channel'] as String)?.trim() ?: 'unknown'
    def kafka_topic    = (props['vm_key']         as String)?.trim() ?: 'n/a'
    def vm_error       = (props['vm_error']        as String)?.trim() ?: 'none'
    def timestamp      = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    def error_map = [
        header: [
            script  : 'acme.core.script.kafka.router',
            author  : 'Ricardo Viana - SAP BTP Integration Expert',
            severity: 'error'
        ],
        input: [
            kafka_topic: kafka_topic
        ],
        routing: [
            timestamp     : timestamp,
            vm_key        : kafka_topic,
            target_channel: target_channel,
            is_mapped     : false,
            vm_artifact   : 'acme.kafka.domain',
            vm_error      : vm_error
        ],
        action_required: "Register topic '${kafka_topic}' in value mapping 'acme.kafka.domain'"
    ]

    message.setBody(
        groovy.json.JsonOutput.prettyPrint(
            groovy.json.JsonOutput.toJson(error_map)
        )
    )
    message.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json')

    return message
}

Appendix - B

Groovy - Iflow Processing - Inside the Spoiler

I'm going to left to possibilities of output as JSON or XML than you change as per of your needs, the sample I used XML as output because of that JSON is commented.

Spoiler
// =============================================================================
//  SAP Community Blog
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  title   : Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI
//            — Domain-Driven Event Routing for Sales O2C Topics
//  author  : Ricardo Viana — SAP BTP Integration Architect | SDIA Creator
//  series  : SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
//  pattern : ODCP — Operational Domain Contract Pattern
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  abstract:
//    This script demonstrates how to implement a domain-scoped Kafka topic
//    router inside SAP Cloud Integration using the SDIA governance model.
//    The AVRO deserialization is decoupled from the input source — the same
//    logic handles raw bytes from the Kafka Sender Adapter, Base64, or Hex,
//    with only a single line change depending on the scenario.
//    The AVRO schema is externalized via CPI Externalized Parameters,
//    making the iFlow immutable — no code change required when the schema
//    evolves.
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  related artifacts:
//    value mapping : acme.kafka.domain / acme.kafka.topic
//    ext parameter : avro (AVRO schema JSON)
//    iflow package : ACME.Sales.O2C
// =============================================================================

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import org.apache.avro.Schema
import org.apache.avro.generic.GenericDatumReader
import org.apache.avro.generic.GenericRecord
import org.apache.avro.io.BinaryDecoder
import org.apache.avro.io.DecoderFactory
import groovy.json.JsonBuilder
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder
import java.io.ByteArrayInputStream

def Message processData(Message message) {

    // ── 1. INPUT — choose according to your scenario ──────────────────────────
    //
    //  PRODUCTION (Kafka Sender Adapter — raw bytes):
        byte[] rawBytes = message.getBody(byte[].class)
    //
    //  SIMULATION — Base64 string (e.g. Content Modifier):
    //  byte[] rawBytes =  Base64.getDecoder().decode(message.getBody(String.class).trim())
    //
    //  SIMULATION — Hex string (e.g. Content Modifier):
    //  def hex = message.getBody(String.class).trim().replaceAll("\\s+", "")
    //  byte[] rawBytes = hex.replaceAll("[^0-9a-fA-F]", "").toList().collate(2)
    //                       .collect { it.join() }
    //                       .collect { Integer.parseInt(it, 16) as byte }
    //                       .toArray(new byte[0])
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    // ── Confluent wire format detection ───────────────────────────────────────
    // Confluent prepends: [ 0x00 magic ][ 4 bytes schema ID ][ avro payload ]
    // If detected, strip the first 5 bytes before deserializing.
    byte[] avroBytes
    if (rawBytes[0] == 0x00) {
        avroBytes = rawBytes[5..-1] as byte[]
    } else {
        avroBytes = rawBytes
    }
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    // ── 2. Schema via Externalized Parameter (iFlow immutable) ────────────────
    def schemaStr = message.getProperty("avro").toString()

    // ── 3. Deserialize AVRO → GenericRecord ───────────────────────────────────
    Schema schema = new Schema.Parser().parse(schemaStr)
    ByteArrayInputStream bais = new ByteArrayInputStream(avroBytes)
    BinaryDecoder decoder = DecoderFactory.get().binaryDecoder(bais, null)
    GenericDatumReader reader = new GenericDatumReader(schema)
    GenericRecord decoded = (GenericRecord) reader.read(null, decoder)

    /*// ── 4. GenericRecord → JSON ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    def builder = new JsonBuilder()
    builder {
        orderId     decoded.get("orderId").toString()
        customerId  decoded.get("customerId").toString()
        totalAmount decoded.get("totalAmount")
        currency    decoded.get("currency").toString()
        status      decoded.get("status").toString()
        createdAt   decoded.get("createdAt")
        items       decoded.get("items").collect { item -> [
            sku     : item.get("sku").toString(),
            quantity: item.get("quantity"),
            price   : item.get("price")
        ]}
    }*/
    
    // ── 5. GenericRecord → XML ────────────────────────────────────────────────
    Writer writer = new StringWriter()
    def indentPrinter = new IndentPrinter(writer, ' ')
    def builder = new MarkupBuilder(indentPrinter)

    
    builder.Order {
        orderId     decoded.get("orderId").toString()
        customerId  decoded.get("customerId").toString()
        totalAmount decoded.get("totalAmount")
        currency    decoded.get("currency").toString()
        status      decoded.get("status").toString()
        createdAt   decoded.get("createdAt")
        Items {
            decoded.get("items").each { item ->
                Item {
                    sku      item.get("sku").toString()
                    quantity item.get("quantity")
                    price    item.get("price")
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    message.setBody(writer.toString())
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/xml")

    //message.setBody(builder.toPrettyString())
    //message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

    return message
}

Appendix - C

Required JAR

Upload to iFlow under Resources → Archives (JAR):

Library Version Source
avro-1.12.1.jar1.12.1https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/org/apache/avro/avro/1.12.1/

Appendix - D

Sample of avro and schema

Dinary base64 sample input to Kafka Topic

{
  "value": {
    "type": "BINARY",
    "data": "HE9SRC0yMDI0LTAwMTIzEENVU1QtNDU2AAAAAABzl0AGQlJMAICgq4f0YwQOTUFULTAwMQYAAAAAAOR1QA5NQVQtMDAyAgAAAAAAIHxAAA=="
  }
}

Avro Schema - inside the Spoiler:

Spoiler
{
  "type": "record",
  "name": "Order",
  "namespace": "com.example.sap",
  "fields": [
    {
      "name": "orderId",
      "type": "string"
    },
    {
      "name": "customerId",
      "type": "string"
    },
    {
      "name": "totalAmount",
      "type": "double"
    },
    {
      "name": "currency",
      "type": "string"
    },
    {
      "name": "status",
      "type": {
        "type": "enum",
        "name": "OrderStatus",
        "symbols": [
          "CREATED",
          "CONFIRMED",
          "CANCELLED"
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "name": "createdAt",
      "type": "long"
    },
    {
      "name": "items",
      "type": {
        "type": "array",
        "items": {
          "type": "record",
          "name": "OrderItem",
          "fields": [
            {
              "name": "sku",
              "type": "string"
            },
            {
              "name": "quantity",
              "type": "int"
            },
            {
              "name": "price",
              "type": "double"
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Event Routing per Domain with Metadata-Driven Consumer Orchestration
entry.content:
"One adapter. One router. Value Mapping entries. The domain decides consumption. Metadata decides behavior."

Introduction

After publishing the previous Kafka Topic routing blog , an interesting architectural question was raised via LinkedIn DM:

"How can SAP CPI control single consumers, multiple consumers, or both simultaneously for the same Kafka topic while still reading the topic with wildcard through only one Kafka Sender Adapter and one inbound channel?"

That question exposed a real gap.

The previous implementation demonstrated immutable routing per domain, but not dynamic consumer orchestration.

This blog introduces a metadata-driven Smart Router capable of resolving consumer behavior at runtime using Value Mapping and Groovy logic.

The result:

One Kafka topic per Domain/subdomain
→ One immutable router per domain/subdomain
→ N consumers resolved dynamically via metadata

without changing the producer, without changing the adapter, without redeploying the router.

Transport-Agnostic Routing Principle

Although this implementation uses the SAP Kafka Sender Adapter, the routing pattern itself is transport-agnostic.

Modern brokers like Solace (SAP AEM), RabbitMQ, or Azure Service Bus support native topic wildcards (*, >), which means a single inbound adapter can capture a wide stream of domain events. In those cases, the value of the Smart Router increases — because the alternative is polluting the iFlow with endless native Content-Based Routers.

The pattern depends only on:

  • an inbound event identifier
  • semantic metadata
  • consumer resolution logic

So the same model applies to:

  • SAP Event Mesh (EM/AEM), Solace, RabbitMQ
  • AMQP, MQTT, HTTPS/Webhooks
  • Event-driven REST endpoints

The transport changes. The routing principle does not:

Inbound Event

→ Metadata Resolution
→ Consumer Strategy
→ Domain Processing

However, the architectural pattern itself is not tied to Kafka.

⚠️This is a reference template, not a production-ready blueprint.

The principle is the deliverable. The implementation is your decision.

Scope and Governance Notice

This pattern follows SDIA / ODCP / EDCP semantic naming conventions. If your organization uses different standards, adapt topics, queues, packages, iFlows, Value Mappings, ProcessDirect URLs, and credentials accordingly. The routing logic itself is naming-independent.

Schema modeling, retries, DLQ, security hardening, mappings, and operational guardrails were intentionally simplified — this is a minimal proof of concept demonstrating immutable event routing.

This blog is the continuation of:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI

Reference blog for IDOC:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice

Reference blog for APIM:

SAP BTP - API Management with SDIA — GDCR & DDCR in Practice

SAP BTP - APIM - DDCR - Deterministic Routing Mechanism JavaScript Policy full explanation each step

The Real Architectural Problem

Traditional multi-consumer implementations usually introduce:

  • duplicated iFlows
  • additional adapters
  • hardcoded routing
  • custom producer headers
  • JMS orchestration
  • CPI-side retry logic

As a result, the producer starts knowing too much about downstream processing.

Kafka was designed to decouple producers from consumers. The producer should emit an event; the domain decides how the event is consumed.

Why custom headers and kafka.PARTITION were rejected

Headers such as x-routing-mode, x-consumer-count, or x-target-system would force the producer to know downstream orchestration details, violating producer–consumer decoupling.

kafka.PARTITION was also rejected because partition metadata is infrastructure-related, not semantic. Partitions may change due to rebalance, scaling, retention, or topic redesign. They cannot be used as a stable routing contract.

This implementation therefore uses:

Topic → Value Mapping → Consumer Strategy

The topic represents business intent. The metadata defines consumer behavior.

That is why the routing responsibility belongs to the domain — not to the producer.

Scalability: Why Centralizing the Inbound Matters

The Smart Router pattern does not eliminate processing iFlows. It eliminates duplicated inbound infrastructure.

In a domain-centric SDIA architecture, processing iFlows still exist because business responsibilities remain separated by domain behavior. That is governance — not sprawl.

Realistic example — Topics

Example - Topics:

  • acme.sales.o2c.order.created
  • acme.sales.o2c.order.confirmed
  • acme.sales.o2c.order.cancelled
  • acme.sales.o2c.invoice.created
 Traditional approach Smart Router
Kafka Sender Adapters41
Credentials41
Inbound iFlows41 immutable router
Processing iFlows44
Routing logicIflow spaguette with local Routing based content.metadata-driven
New consumernew iFlow + deployedit one VM row

The key distinction:

  • Processing separation = governance
  • Inbound adapter duplication = operational sprawl

The gain is not reducing all iFlows to one. The gain is freezing the inbound footprint while letting downstream processing evolve independently through metadata.

As new consumers appear, no new inbound adapters or router iFlows are required.

Only metadata evolves.

That reduces:

  • adapter proliferation
  • duplicated orchestration
  • deployment overhead
  • routing maintenance
  • credential rotation
  • operational TCO

while preserving domain ownership and decoupling.

The first model demonstrates the traditional integration approach: one adapter, one iFlow or consumer combination.

As event consumption grows, the landscape quickly evolves into duplicated orchestration, fragmented governance, and operational sprawl.

The second model centralizes routing responsibility inside a single immutable Smart Router per domain. Consumer behavior becomes metadata-driven instead of hardcoded, reducing adapter proliferation while preserving domain ownership and decoupling.

The Three Architectural Possible Models

Before implementing the solution, three architectures were designed and validated.

Only one became the recommended model.

Architecture 1 — Two Layers: Simple Router + Consumer Dispatcher

 

LayerResponsibility
Kafka AdapterReads topics via wildcard
Router iFlowResolves metadata existence
Dispatcher iFlowDecides single / multi / both / fallback
Processing iFlowsBusiness processing per consumer
  • Pros: clean responsibility separation, isolated dispatcher. 
  • Cons: extra ProcessDirect hop, more artifacts, higher latency.

Elegant, but increases artifact proliferation.

Architecture 2 — Single Layer: All-in-One Smart Router ✅ Recommended

 

LayerResponsibility
Kafka AdapterReads domain topics via wildcard
Immutable Smart RouterMetadata resolution + consumer strategy + dispatch
Processing iFlowsDomain business processing
  • Pros: fewer artifacts, simpler governance, immutable router, metadata-driven. 
  • Cons: router becomes denser; multicast/splitter orchestration is centralized.

The complexity becomes centralized rather than distributed across duplicated iFlows. 

This is the architecture implemented in this blog.

Architecture 3 — Three Layers: Global Event Router + Decision Layer ❌ Not Recommended as Default

A single router reading all topics from all domains was tested and deliberately excluded:

  • single point of impact for every domain
  • shared dependency violates SDIA domain autonomy
  • additional hops and latency
  • changes in the global router affect everything

The boundary belongs to the domain. The router must live inside it.

Trade-offs of This Pattern

To be honest about what you're adopting:

  • The router becomes denser — debugging multicast/splitter paths requires familiarity with the engine.
  • The pattern depends on semantic discipline in the Value Mapping. Sloppy metadata = sloppy routing.
  • Governance only holds if the team respects the domain boundary. The framework formalizes it; humans still have to honor it.
  • Validation of physical ProcessDirect endpoints is delegated to CPI runtime — a misconfigured VM entry is detected at execution, not at deploy.

Why the Router Owns Consumer Orchestration

The SAP Kafka Sender Adapter does not provide dynamic consumer orchestration. The router absorbs:

  • metadata resolution
  • consumer strategy
  • dispatch orchestration

Business processing remains inside the processing iFlows. The router decides how an event is consumed; the domain iFlows decide what the event means.

Why two Value Mapping contracts

The implementation separates:

  • Single Consumer Mapping
  • Multiple Consumer Mapping

Single routing is deterministic; multi routing requires iteration; “both” requires multicast. Splitting the contracts keeps the orchestration logic and the metadata readable.

Kafka retry behavior — NACK by design

Iflow ResultKafka Behavior
SuccessOffset committed
Error End EventOffset retained
 

FALLBACK intentionally raises an Error End Event, so Kafka retries the message later. Kafka already acts as the retry engine — adding JMS or Data Store retry loops would duplicate a responsibility Kafka already solves natively.

Allowed Headers — Critical Configuration

Before deploying, add the following to the Kafka Sender Adapter Allowed Headers field:

kafka.TOPIC

Without this, the kafka.TOPIC header is not propagated to the iFlow and the router receives an empty topic key — every message falls to FALLBACK.

You can also expose for observability (optional):

kafka.TOPIC, kafka.PARTITION, kafka.OFFSET or kafka.*

The Event Routing iFlow — Architecture 2 in CI

This implementation centralizes metadata resolution, consumer orchestration, and dispatching inside a single immutable Smart Router per domain, keeping the inbound layer stable while allowing downstream consumers to evolve independently through metadata.

Flow steps

  1. Kafka Sender — inbound Kafka Sender Adapter, wildcard topic, single channel.

  2. Groovy — Metadata lookup — resolves the topic against the Value Mapping (single + multi consumer contracts) and dynamically builds the ProcessDirect targets; preserves the original AVRO payload as Base64.

  3. Router Guard the entrance of DOMAIN LAYER  — evaluates is_mapped. If no mapping is found → Fallback branch.

  4. Router — One Consumer or XConsumer — evaluates is_single and is_xconsumer and selects the routing state: SINGLE, XCONS, BOTH, or FALLBACK.

  5. Single branch — Restore AVRO payload — Groovy restores the original Kafka AVRO payload before dispatching.

  6. Multiple Consumers branch — Build target channels — sets the target_channel property and prepares the multi-target list for iteration.

  7. Both branch — Parallel Multicast — fans out the message in parallel to the SINGLE path and the XCONS path simultaneously.

  8. Splitter — Multiple consumers from same topic — iterates over the resolved target channels (one dispatch per consumer).

  9. Restore payload + End — each branch restores the original AVRO payload before reaching its End event, ensuring multicast/splitter consistency.

  10. ProcessDirect Receiver — all branches dispatch to the corresponding domain processing iFlows via ProcessDirect.

Fallback path

  • Fallback — No Routing found — when neither contract resolves, the Groovy step Build exception response generates a structured JSON error and terminates at Error End 2 (offset is not committed → Kafka retries).

The Processing Iflow Topic — Architecture 2 in CI

After the Smart Router dispatches the event via ProcessDirect, the processing iFlow consumes the frozen AVRO payload, applies the schema contract, and converts it into business-readable XML or JSON.

  1.  Avro Schema (Content Modifier)
    • The AVRO schema is loaded as an Externalized Parameter directly into the iFlow, avoiding external lookups and keeping the processing flow immutable and lightweight.
  2.  Groovy: AVRO → JSON/XML
    • The Groovy script reads the binary AVRO payload, applies the schema contract, and converts the event into JSON or XML.

⚠️This is a reference template only. Schemas, mappings, output formats, and processing logic must be adapted to your own integration landscape.

Why the AVRO Schema Uses Externalized Parameters

The AVRO schema was intentionally stored as an Externalized Parameter via Content Modifier instead of Partner Directory, Data Store, or JMS.

Why ?

  • Partner Directory → designed for partner/pipeline configuration, not domain event contracts
  • Data Store → unnecessary database I/O for static schema reads
  • JMS → overlaps with Kafka buffering responsibilities

Keeping the schema as an Externalized Parameter, the iFlow and Groovy logic remain immutable while avoiding external lookups, hidden dependencies, and additional runtime overhead.

This pattern is optimized for small to medium schemas.

For enterprise-scale schema lifecycle governance and compatibility management, a dedicated Schema Registry remains the recommended approach.

The Architectural Trade-Off: High-Throughput Memory Footprint

While Base64 guarantees total payload protection, enterprise architects must account for underlying JVM Heap memory mechanics:

  • Size Inflation: The Base64 encoding algorithm increases the original file size by approximately 33%.
  • RAM Multiplier: When creating the Base64 property, the JVM must concurrently allocate the original byte[], the newly spawned String object, and the temporary buffers needed for the conversion.

Under very large payloads (10MB–20MB+) and heavy parallelism, this may increase the risk of java.lang.OutOfMemoryError (OOM).

This pattern remains safe here because the architecture intentionally enforces sequential FIFO consumption, allowing predictable JVM Heap and Garbage Collector behavior.

For very large payloads under high concurrency, streaming InputStream processing is the recommended alternative.

Single Groovy Script Architecture — Optimizing JVM Execution & Cache

A common anti-pattern in SAP CPI design is "Visual Script Fragmentation"—breaking down integration logic into successive, individual Groovy script steps across the iFlow canvas. While visually modular, this approach introduces unnecessary JVM resource overhead.

This architecture deliberately centralizes the entire semantic routing logic, splitter preparation, and error formatting into one single Groovy file powered by internal private functions:

def Message processData(Message message) { ... }
def Message restoreOriginal(Message message) { ... }
def Message buildErrorPayload(Message message) { ... }

Performance Breakdown & CPU Footprint Optimization

  • Warm Cache Compilation: When an iFlow is initialized, the Camel runtime reads the .groovy file, compiles it into a Java bytecode class (.class), and caches it inside the JVM’s Metaspace memory layer. By using a single script file, the JVM compiles and caches exactly one class for the entire routing execution.
  • Nano-Second Method Execution: When the iFlow moves through different stages (Routing, Iteration, Fallback) and calls the respective internal functions, the execution pointer remains inside the exact same class instance already resident in the CPU cache.
  • Eliminating Context Switching: If the logic were scattered across three different script steps, the JVM would be forced to load, instantiate, and manage three distinct classes. Moving from one script step to another introduces a measurable processing overhead (context switching and local variable scoping stack re-allocation).

Key Architectural Takeaways Highlighted by the Diagram:

  • Left — Anti-Pattern:
    Multiple fragmented Groovy steps increase JVM overhead through additional class loading, context switching, and fragmented execution flow.
  • Right — Optimized Smart Router:
    A single Groovy class with internal helper methods keeps execution inside one cached runtime footprint, reducing overhead and keeping the routing engine lightweight and predictable.

Testing Analyses:

Single consumer:

 

N consumer:

Both - Single and N consumer:
